files.name: bfs.algo
files.content:
bfsInit()

while not queue.isEmpty() do
  u ← queue.dequeue()
  visit(u)
  for each v in neighbors(u) do
    discover(u, v)
  end
end

reconstructPath(goal())

notes.text: Set up the search in one step — create the **queue**, the **dist** and **prev** maps and the **visited** set, then seed them from the Start vertex. Keeping setup in a helper lets the main loop read like the algorithm itself.
BFS explores the graph in waves. The **queue** holds the *frontier*: vertices found but not yet expanded. We loop until it drains.
Take the next vertex from the **front** of the queue. First-in, first-out order is what makes the search breadth-first — nearer vertices are always expanded before farther ones.
Settle `u`. Thanks to the FIFO order, the first time BFS reaches a vertex it has already found its shortest distance, so `visit` can label it as final.
Fan out to every neighbour of `u` — the candidates for the next wave of the search.
`discover` adds `v` to the frontier the first time it is seen, recording `dist[u] + 1` and remembering `u` as its predecessor. Already-seen vertices are skipped.
Walk the **prev** map backward from the Goal to the Start to light up the shortest path. `reconstructPath` lives in the shared **helpers** file — any search that records predecessors can reuse it.

files.name: bfs-helpers.algo
files.content:
export function bfsInit() do
  queue ← panel.createQueue("queue")
  dist ← panel.createMap("dist")
  prev ← panel.createMap("prev")
  visited ← panel.createSet("visited")
  s ← source()
  dist[s] ← 0
  visited.add(s)
  queue.enqueue(s)
  mark(s, "info")
  scrollTo(s)
  setLabel(s, 0)
  showMessage("Start BFS from " + s)
end

export function visit(u) do
  mark(u, "success")
  scrollTo(u)
  setLabel(u, dist[u])
  showMessage("Visiting " + u + " (distance " + dist[u] + ")")
end

export function discover(u, v) do
  if v in visited then return end
  dist[v] ← dist[u] + 1
  prev[v] ← u
  visited.add(v)
  queue.enqueue(v)
  mark(u, v, "info")
  setLabel(v, dist[v])
  showMessage("Found " + v + " from " + u)
end

notes.text: Algorithm-specific setup. The four structures are created off-canvas with `panel.*`, so they stay out of the drawing but still appear in the Run data panel, where you can watch them fill.
The frontier. BFS pushes to the back and pulls from the front, so vertices leave in the order they were discovered.
Highlight the vertex BFS is expanding and pin its final distance on the canvas. Pure visualization — the result is the same without it, but the run is far easier to follow.
The heart of BFS: each vertex is discovered exactly once, from the first vertex that reaches it.
The guard that keeps BFS linear — skip any vertex already seen so none is ever enqueued twice.
A BFS distance is just a hop count: one more than the vertex we arrived from.

files.name: helpers.algo
files.content:
export function reconstructPath(t) do
  showMessage("Tracing the path back to the Start", "success")
  mark(t, "warn")
  cur ← t
  while cur in prev do
    p ← prev[cur]
    mark(p, cur, "warn")
    mark(p, "warn")
    scrollTo(p, cur)
    cur ← p
  end
  showMessage("Shortest path highlighted", "success")
end

notes.text: A **general** helper, not tied to BFS. It reads the global **prev** map — the predecessor each vertex was reached from — so any search that records predecessors (BFS, Dijkstra, A*) can call it to draw a path. This is also where shared utilities like `isDAG` would live.
Follow predecessors from the target back toward the Start. The Start has no predecessor, so `cur in prev` turns false there and the loop stops.
Re-colour each edge along the route so the shortest path stands out from the rest of the BFS tree.
